items.q: Is water scarcity in a country always due to low rainfall?
items.correct: No — economic scarcity occurs where rainfall is adequate but storage, treatment and distribution infrastructure are lacking; physical scarcity is about the supply itself.
items.misconception: Only dry countries face water insecurity.
items.random_wrong: Water scarcity happens when reservoirs are painted dark colours.
items.why: The physical versus economic distinction is the analytical heart of resource management.

items.q: Does importing much of its food mean the UK has no food security?
items.correct: Not necessarily — diverse import sources spread risk and the UK still produces a substantial share of its food, though import reliance does raise exposure to price shocks and supply disruption.
items.misconception: Food imports mean a country has zero food security.
items.why: Students need the balanced view: imports create vulnerability but also diversification.

items.q: Is energy security only about how much energy a country produces?
items.correct: No — it also involves affordability, reliability of imports, diversity of the energy mix and managing demand; a big producer can still be insecure if supply is undiversified.
items.misconception: Producing lots of energy automatically makes a country energy secure.
items.random_wrong: Energy security means guarding power stations with fences.
items.why: Multi-dimensional definitions earn the marks on resource security questions.

items.q: What is the Hubbert curve about?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Peak oil. “Population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
It's South America. “Africa” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What % of world lithium is in the Lithium Triangle?
items.why: Exact answer: 60 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
